Claim: Full model tracks recent monthly unemployment with sub-pp RMSE on the post-COVID window.
Caveat: 5-seed mean only; cross-seed SD 0.023 pp; dynamic multi-step not rolling.
Paper-ready wording: Over 2022-01 to 2026-02 the recalibrated V_Full attains UR RMSE 0.27 pp (5-seed mean, SD 0.02), bias −0.04 pp, correlation 0.79.

Claim: Model performance varies materially across evaluation windows.
Caveat: Cross-window differences are not equalised by any window-specific re-fit.
Paper-ready wording: The model attains UR RMSE 0.27 pp on the post-COVID window but 2.82 pp on the 2020-03..2021-12 crisis window without re-fit; both numbers are reported together.

Claim: The post-COVID window is the headline tracking window of the paper.
Caveat: Crisis-window number must also be reported.
Paper-ready wording: We treat the post-COVID 2022-01..2026-02 window as the headline tracking window; the COVID crisis window is reported as a boundary diagnostic.

Claim: Full model improves over separately calibrated internal controls.
Caveat: All three controls are separately recalibrated under the same LHS budget.
Paper-ready wording: Under the same calibration budget, V_Full attains UR RMSE 0.27 pp vs 0.36 (LaborOnly), 0.55 (Homogeneous), 0.56 (Simplified).

Claim: The heterogeneity advantage is moderate
Caveat: Moderate
Paper-ready wording: The 0.29 pp total gain is within the noise band of the strongest external benchmarks.
column10: The total RMSE gain of V_Full over the flattened reference is 0.29 pp on the post-COVID window.

Claim: The source-of-advantage decomposition is an accounting identity
Caveat: Strong (definitional)
Paper-ready wording: Each variant is recalibrated; some difference is absorbed by parameter substitution.
column10: We refer to the 94 / 6 percent split as a within-experiment accounting decomposition rather than a causal partition.

Claim: Search-friction heterogeneity is the least substitutable channel.
Caveat: Magnitude is an accounting delta under recalibration
column10: Recalibrating after removing search-friction heterogeneity leaves the largest residual gap (UR RMSE 1.09 pp, +0.81 pp vs V_Full).

Claim: Liquidity and housing dimensions are largely substitutable after recalibration.
Caveat: V_NoHousing slightly improving over V_Full is an artefact of parameter reassignment.
Paper-ready wording: V_NoLiquidity attains 0.38 pp (+0.11) and V_NoHousing attains 0.24 pp (−0.04) after recalibration.

Claim: The heterogeneity ladder confirms incremental marginal value of adding dimensions.
Caveat: Order-dependent; several adjacent levels differ by less than the seed SD.
Paper-ready wording: Starting from a homogeneous L0 baseline at 0.55 pp, incrementally adding heterogeneity dimensions monotonically improves UR RMSE down to 0.27 pp at L6, with the largest single-step gain at the housing step.

Claim: ABM is competitive with external benchmarks under the same dynamic protocol.
Caveat: ABM trains on 2004-2017 while benchmarks train on 2001-2022.
Paper-ready wording: Under a common dynamic multi-step protocol on the post-COVID window, the ABM ranks first across twelve models.

Claim: ABM advantage over the best simple benchmarks is modest.
Caveat: Margin is the same order as the ABM's own seed SD.
Paper-ready wording: The margin over the closest benchmarks (No-change and ETS, both at 0.31 pp) is 0.04 pp — comparable to the ABM's cross-seed standard deviation of 0.02 pp.

Claim: Rolling one-step benchmarks are reported as a diagnostic
Caveat: Moderate
Paper-ready wording: The dynamic multi-step protocol is the headline protocol throughout the paper.
column10: We report rolling-origin one-step results as a sensitivity to the forecasting protocol, not as the headline benchmark comparison.

Claim: Results are stable across evaluation seeds.
Caveat: Five final evaluation seeds {42
column13: Five evaluation seeds yield a cross-seed standard deviation of 0.02 pp in the post-COVID UR RMSE.

Claim: Results are not driven by one training window.
Caveat: Computed on the legacy M0 family; ranking-level conclusion.
Paper-ready wording: Across ten alternative train/test splits, the ABM family wins against the four legacy benchmarks on six of the seven post-COVID-aligned splits.

Claim: Results are not driven by agent count.
Caveat: Plateau identified at N ≥ 50
column10: Mean UR RMSE plateaus for N ≥ 50,000 agents; the default N = 100,000 is past the plateau.

Claim: Results are not driven by one calibration method.
Caveat: Performance CV measured at 5.55% across five methods.
Paper-ready wording: Holding the simulator fixed and varying the calibration method across five protocols produces best-test UR RMSE in the band 0.21 to 0.24 pp (CV 5.55 percent).

Claim: Many free parameters are weakly identified.
Caveat: 10 of 14 parameters exhibit CV ≥ 0.40 across top-5 within at least one method.
Paper-ready wording: Ten of fourteen free parameters exhibit a coefficient of variation greater than 0.40 across the top-five candidates within a single calibration method.

Claim: Parameter values are fitted simulation quantities
Caveat: Strong (methodological)
Paper-ready wording: This is a wording rule and applies across the paper; do not say "structurally identifies" for the 10 weak parameters.
column10: We report all calibrated parameters as fitted simulation quantities reported as bands, not as structurally identified estimates.

Claim: The model is predictive and diagnostic
Caveat: Strong (framing)
Paper-ready wording: This is a positioning claim; it constrains how E6/E7/E8 decompositions can be described.
column10: We position the model as a survey-moment-anchored predictive and diagnostic tool, not as a structurally identified model.

Claim: The model does not claim universal accuracy across regimes.
Caveat: Crisis-window error 2.82 pp is reported as a boundary.
Paper-ready wording: We do not claim universal accuracy across regimes; the COVID crisis window error of 2.82 pp marks the current boundary of the model's quantitative reach.
